mechanism: SOC formation depends on soil biota breaking down organic litter, and in turn the quantity/quality of SOC determines how much microbial activity the soil can support — a two-way feedback, not a one-way cause.
intervention: Increase SOC inputs via organic amendments or reduced tillage to support microbial community recovery
estimated_effect: qualitative — no percentage given in source
source: FAO 2017, Soil Organic Carbon: The Hidden Potential, Section 2.2, p.12

trigger.condition: intensive monoculture heavy tillage chemical inputs

mechanism: Intensive land management pushes soils toward a low-biodiversity state, which increases pest/pathogen pressure and decreases beneficial organisms — shown as a branching pathway in the source's own figure.
intervention: Shift toward reduced-intensity management: crop rotation, reduced tillage, lower chemical input

mechanism: Earthworms and millipedes physically break down and translocate organic matter to deeper soil layers, where it has a longer residence time before decomposing back to CO2 — low SOC is both a symptom and a driver of reduced macrofauna activity.
intervention: Practices that support macrofauna populations (reduced tillage, organic residue retention)
estimated_effect: qualitative — mechanism-level, no percentage in source

mechanism: Cover cropping and crop rotation increase the amount and continuity of photosynthetic carbon input to soil, supporting SOC accumulation over conventional monoculture.
intervention: Introduce cover crops and/or crop rotation
estimated_effect: directional increase; FAO cites this as evidence-backed but does not state a bundled percentage in this document
source: FAO 2017, Figure 13, p.49 (cites Poeplau & Don 2015 for cover cropping, Raphael et al. 2016 for rotations)

mechanism: Organic matter improves soil aggregate and structural stability, which increases porosity and water infiltration, reducing runoff and improving drought buffering.
intervention: Raise SOC through organic amendments to improve water retention

mechanism: Lower rainfall and rising temperature increase aridity and evapotranspiration, which reduces soil moisture; lower soil moisture reduces both SOC and plant productivity (NPP), which further degrades soil and expands dryland conditions — a self-reinforcing feedback loop.
intervention: Interventions that raise soil water retention (mulching, cover crops, agroforestry canopy shade) to interrupt the feedback loop
estimated_effect: Average temperature increase in drylands is roughly 1.8x greater than in humid regions

mechanism: Land-use change is identified as the direct driver with the single largest relative negative impact on terrestrial and freshwater ecosystems since 1970, ahead of direct exploitation, pollution, and climate change.
intervention: Prioritize land-use interventions (reduce further conversion, restore degraded land) over other single levers when addressing terrestrial biodiversity loss
estimated_effect: Over one-third of the terrestrial land surface is used for cropping or animal husbandry
source: IPBES Global Assessment SPM 2019, point B1

mechanism: Land degradation directly reduces terrestrial productivity. Independently, pollinator decline (a biodiversity indicator) puts a large share of pollinator-dependent crop output at risk, since most leading global food crops depend at least partly on animal pollination.
intervention: Sustainable land management + pollinator-supporting practices (habitat strips, reduced pesticide use, agroforestry)
estimated_effect: Land degradation has reduced productivity across 23% of global terrestrial area; between $235-577 billion in annual global crop output is at risk from pollinator loss
source: IPBES Global Assessment SPM 2019, point A3

mechanism: Widespread human alteration of land has driven a long-term decline in native species abundance across most terrestrial biomes, with the decline concentrated since 1900 and potentially accelerating.
intervention: Habitat restoration and reduced land conversion
estimated_effect: Average abundance of native species in most terrestrial biomes has fallen by at least 20% since 1900
source: IPBES Global Assessment SPM 2019, point A4

mechanism: Species richness scales with structural and floral complexity of the land-use system — continuous monoculture supports far fewer species per plot than agroforestry, which in turn supports fewer than primary forest.
intervention: Convert monoculture plots to agroforestry/intercropping systems to raise on-plot species richness
estimated_effect: Per 1.5-ha plot in Jambi, Sumatra: 15 species in continuous cassava monoculture, 25 in oil palm plantation, 90 in rubber agroforestry, 120 in primary forest (Murdiyarso et al., cited in source)

mechanism: Species richness in home-garden agroforestry systems increases with the size of the garden/patch — a direct instance of the species-area relationship central to habitat fragmentation ecology.
intervention: Where possible, consolidate or expand green patch size rather than fragmenting into smaller disconnected plots
estimated_effect: Tropical home garden species richness ranged from 27 species (Sri Lanka) to 602 species (West Java), size-correlated (Bardhan et al., cited in source)

mechanism: Compared to intact forest, monoculture cropping causes a much larger drop in pollinator diversity than mixed-tree agroforestry does, because agroforestry retains more of the vegetation structure, nesting sites, and floral resources pollinators depend on.
intervention: Convert monoculture cropland to mixed-tree agroforestry / intercropping to retain pollinator populations
estimated_effect: Pollinator decline relative to forest baseline: 40% under monocrops vs. 15% under mixed-tree agroforestry; mixed-tree AF retained 85-93% of forest pollinator levels (Barroios et al., cited in source)

mechanism: Tree integration in cropland provides nesting sites, food sources, and structural complexity that row-crop monoculture lacks, directly increasing bird species richness over time.
intervention: Introduce tree-based intercropping or windbreaks into row-crop systems
estimated_effect: Avian species richness nearly doubled, from 17 to 32 species, over 1995-2014 in a Canadian tree-based intercropping system (Gibbs et al., cited in source)

mechanism: The higher root density and diversity of vegetation (trees, shrubs, grass, crops) in agroforestry systems increases belowground biomass input and root-associated microbial habitat, raising bacterial abundance and richness relative to monoculture.
intervention: Integrate trees/shrubs into cropland to raise belowground microbial diversity
estimated_effect: Significantly greater bacterial abundance and species richness observed in agroforestry hedgerows/woodlands vs. agricultural land across a 270km soil-climate gradient (Banarjee et al., cited in source)

mechanism: Higher insect and bird biodiversity in agroforestry systems supports natural enemies of crop pests (ants, parasitoids, insectivorous birds), suppressing pest populations without chemical input.
intervention: Adopt agroforestry coffee/cocoa systems to support natural pest-control fauna
estimated_effect: Coffee berry borer infestation was 69% lower in agroforestry coffee than in monocrop coffee in West Africa (cited in source)
